title.en: FinOps Across Clouds: One Cost Model to Rule Them All

description.en: Normalize three billing dialects into one allocation model: FOCUS-based cost data, tagging discipline, commitment strategy, and unit economics across Azure, AWS, and GCP.

story_variants.en:
## Three Invoices, One Question

The CFO asks the only question that matters: *"What does the checkout service cost us per thousand orders, and is it trending the right way?"* You have an Azure invoice organized by subscription and resource group, an AWS Cost and Usage Report keyed by account and tags, and a GCP billing export keyed by project and labels. Three currencies of the same information, none of which answers the question by itself.

**FinOps** — the discipline formalized by the FinOps Foundation — is how engineering, finance, and product share one language about cloud spend. In a multi-cloud estate its first job is brutal: *normalization*.

## The Three Billing Dialects

| Concern | Azure | AWS | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Native cost tool | Microsoft Cost Management | Cost Explorer / CUR 2.0 | Cloud Billing reports / BigQuery export |
| Allocation container | Subscription / Resource Group | Account / OU | Project / Folder |
| Metadata mechanism | Tags (+ tag inheritance policies) | Tags (cost allocation tags must be activated) | Labels |
| Commitment discounts | Reservations + Savings Plans (Azure) | Reserved Instances + Savings Plans | Committed Use Discounts (CUDs) |
| Always-on discounts | Hybrid Benefit (licensing) | — | Sustained Use Discounts (automatic) |
| Detailed export | Cost details / exports to storage | CUR to S3 (Parquet) | Billing export to BigQuery |

> **Callout:** The industry answer to this Babel is **FOCUS** — the FinOps Open Cost and Usage Specification. All three hyperscalers now offer FOCUS-format billing exports, giving you common column semantics (billed cost, effective cost, service category) across providers. Adopt it as your canonical schema; treat native formats as ingestion details.

## The Normalization Pipeline

Every serious multi-cloud FinOps practice builds the same pipeline:

1. **Ingest** — CUR/exports land in a lakehouse daily (billing data is *not* real-time; late-arriving adjustments and amortization restatements are normal).
2. **Normalize** — map provider columns to one schema (FOCUS), convert currencies, choose amortized vs actual cost views deliberately.
3. **Allocate** — attribute every dollar to a team/product/environment via tags, labels, and container hierarchy. Shared costs (networking hubs, security tooling, support plans) get an explicit allocation rule — even split, proportional to direct spend, or usage-metered.
4. **Report unit economics** — cost per order, per tenant, per 1K inferences. Absolute spend going up is fine if unit cost is going down; only unit economics tells you which.

## Tagging: The Unsexy Foundation

Allocation quality is capped by metadata quality. The enforceable rules:

- A small, mandatory tag set (owner, product, environment, cost-center) — enforced *preventively*: Azure Policy deny/modify effects, AWS tag policies + SCP require-tag patterns, GCP org policies and project-factory conventions.
- Tag at the container level where possible (subscription/account/project maps to team) so untagged resources still inherit an owner.
- Track an **allocation coverage KPI**: percent of spend attributable to a team. Mature estates run above 90%; below 70% every report is fiction.

> **Callout:** Untagged spend is not a hygiene problem; it is *unaccountable* spend. Whatever cannot be attributed cannot be optimized, negotiated, or defended in budget season.

## Commitments Across Clouds: The Portfolio Problem

Commitment discounts (reservations, savings plans, CUDs) trade flexibility for discount depth. Multi-cloud makes this a portfolio-risk exercise:

- Commit per cloud based on that cloud's *stable baseline*, never on aggregate spend — a commitment on Azure is worthless when the workload moves to AWS.
- Multi-cloud ambitions quietly reduce optimal commitment levels: mobility and discounts are in direct tension. If you promised the board an exit option, price the discount you are forgoing to keep it.
- Watch utilization: an unused commitment is a 100% loss dressed as a discount. Alert below ~90% utilization.

## Real Failure Modes

- **The egress blind spot.** Cross-cloud data transfer accrues on both sides' invoices under different names; nobody owns it because it belongs to "the network." Give inter-cloud transfer its own budget line and owner.
- **Amortized vs actual confusion.** Finance reads actual (cash) numbers; engineering dashboards show amortized. The two disagree by design, and the meeting dissolves into reconciliation instead of decisions. Pick views per audience, label them loudly.
- **The Kubernetes black hole.** Three clusters show up as three line items of node spend. Without workload-level allocation (e.g., OpenCost-style attribution by namespace/label), the platform team owns everyone's costs and no one's incentives.
- **AI spend surprise.** GPU capacity and per-token model charges (Foundry, Bedrock, Vertex) grow faster than any other category and often bypass tagging conventions entirely. Bring model endpoints and token usage into the same allocation model on day one — you will meet this again in the LLMOps lesson.

## What You Will Build

The challenge is the heart of the pipeline in miniature: three record formats — Azure's resourceGroup/costUSD, AWS's tag_team/unblended_cost, GCP's label_team/cost — normalized into one (team, cost) stream, summed per team, and printed deterministically. Every commercial FinOps platform you will ever buy is, at its core, this loop with more adapters.

description.en: Three billing dialects normalize into one FOCUS-shaped schema, get allocated to teams, and finally answer the CFO's unit-cost question.

left.label: Report absolute spend per cloud, per month

right.label: Report unit economics per product: cost per order, per tenant, per 1K inferences

description.en: Absolute spend rising can be great news or terrible news. Only unit cost tells you which — and it requires allocation to work.

explanation.en: One adapter per provider dialect — exactly what FOCUS standardizes at industry scale.
Azure attributes by resource group and reports costUSD.
AWS uses activated cost-allocation tags and unblended (or amortized) cost columns.
GCP calls the same concept a label. Three names, one meaning: who owns this dollar?

description.en: The multi-cloud FinOps pipeline: every commercial tool you can buy is this flow with more adapters.
Every dollar attributed to a team — the precondition for any optimization conversation.

instruction.en: Build the multi-cloud cost allocator. Implement normalize_records() returning a list of (team, cost) tuples: from AZURE use (resourceGroup, costUSD), from AWS use (tag_team, unblended_cost), from GCP use (label_team, cost) — in that provider order. Implement allocate(rows) returning a dict of team -> summed cost. Print one line per team sorted alphabetically as '<team>: $<amount>' with the amount formatted to 2 decimals, then 'Total spend: $<total>' (2 decimals). This normalize-then-allocate loop is the core of every FinOps platform.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
AZURE = [
    {"resourceGroup": "team-data", "costUSD": 120.50},
    {"resourceGroup": "team-app", "costUSD": 60.00},
]
AWS = [
    {"tag_team": "team-data", "unblended_cost": 80.25},
    {"tag_team": "team-ml", "unblended_cost": 210.25},
]
GCP = [
    {"label_team": "team-app", "cost": 45.00},
    {"label_team": "team-ml", "cost": 39.75},
]

def normalize_records():
    # TODO: return list of (team, cost) tuples from AZURE, then AWS, then GCP
    pass

def allocate(rows):
    # TODO: return dict mapping team -> total cost
    pass

# TODO: print '<team>: $<amount:.2f>' per team, sorted by team name
# TODO: print 'Total spend: $<total:.2f>'

en: normalize_records: three small loops appending tuples — the keys differ per provider, the shape does not.
allocate: totals = {}; for team, cost in rows: totals[team] = totals.get(team, 0) + cost.
Print with f'{team}: ${amount:.2f}' over sorted(totals) and sum the dict values for the total line.

practice_challenges.test_code:
rows = normalize_records()
assert ("team-data", 120.50) in rows and ("team-ml", 210.25) in rows and ("team-app", 45.00) in rows
assert len(rows) == 6
totals = allocate(rows)
assert abs(totals["team-app"] - 105.00) < 1e-9
assert abs(totals["team-data"] - 200.75) < 1e-9
assert abs(totals["team-ml"] - 250.00) < 1e-9
assert allocate([("x", 1.0), ("x", 2.0)]) == {"x": 3.0}

q.en: What problem does the FOCUS specification solve for multi-cloud FinOps?

en: It provides one common schema and column semantics across providers' billing exports
It replaces tags with AI-generated labels

q.en: Why should commitments (RIs, Savings Plans, CUDs) be sized per cloud rather than on aggregate spend?

en: Discounts are only available in USD
A commitment on one cloud is worthless when the workload moves to another — commit to each cloud's stable baseline
Aggregate spend is impossible to compute

q.en: What does low tag/label allocation coverage (e.g., 60% of spend attributable) actually mean?

en: The estate is 40% cheaper than reported
40% of spend is unaccountable — it cannot be optimized, owned, or defended in budget planning

q.en: Why do amortized and actual cost views disagree, and what should you do about it?

en: A billing bug; file a support ticket
By design — upfront commitments are spread over time in amortized views; label the view per audience
Currency conversion errors; always use EUR
